items.q: Write a Python function `def is_palindrome(s: str) -> bool` that returns True if s reads the same forwards and backwards (exact, no whitespace stripping, case-sensitive). Reply with only one ```python code block.
items.tests:
assert is_palindrome('racecar') == True
assert is_palindrome('hello') == False
assert is_palindrome('') == True
assert is_palindrome('a') == True
assert is_palindrome('abba') == True

items.q: Write a Python function `def reverse_words(s: str) -> str` that reverses the order of whitespace-separated words in s (words themselves unchanged). Reply with only one ```python code block.
items.tests:
assert reverse_words('hello world') == 'world hello'
assert reverse_words('a b c') == 'c b a'
assert reverse_words('one') == 'one'

items.q: Write a Python function `def fizzbuzz(n: int) -> list[str]` returning a list for 1..n where multiples of 3 -> 'Fizz', multiples of 5 -> 'Buzz', multiples of both -> 'FizzBuzz', else the number as a string. Reply with only one ```python code block.
items.tests:
assert fizzbuzz(5) == ['1','2','Fizz','4','Buzz']
assert fizzbuzz(15)[14] == 'FizzBuzz'
assert fizzbuzz(3)[2] == 'Fizz'

items.q: Write a Python function `def sum_of_digits(n: int) -> int` that returns the sum of the decimal digits of a non-negative integer n. Reply with only one ```python code block.
items.tests:
assert sum_of_digits(1234) == 10
assert sum_of_digits(0) == 0
assert sum_of_digits(9) == 9

items.q: Write a Python function `def is_prime(n: int) -> bool` that correctly reports primality for any non-negative integer n (0 and 1 are not prime). Reply with only one ```python code block.
items.tests:
assert is_prime(2) == True
assert is_prime(1) == False
assert is_prime(0) == False
assert is_prime(17) == True
assert is_prime(18) == False

items.q: Write a Python function `def gcd(a: int, b: int) -> int` computing the greatest common divisor of two positive integers. Reply with only one ```python code block.
items.tests:
assert gcd(48, 18) == 6
assert gcd(17, 5) == 1
assert gcd(100, 25) == 25

items.q: Write a Python function `def flatten(lst) -> list` that flattens an arbitrarily nested list of ints into a single flat list, preserving order. Reply with only one ```python code block.
Write a Python function `def count_vowels(s: str) -> int` that counts vowels (aeiouAEIOU) in a string. Reply with only one ```python code block.
items.tests:
assert count_vowels('hello world') == 3
assert count_vowels('') == 0
assert count_vowels('AEIOU') == 5

items.q: Write a Python function `def dedupe_preserve_order(lst: list) -> list` that removes duplicate values while keeping the first occurrence's order. Reply with only one ```python code block.
items.tests:
assert dedupe_preserve_order([1,2,2,3,1,4]) == [1,2,3,4]
assert dedupe_preserve_order([]) == []
assert dedupe_preserve_order([1,1,1]) == [1]

items.q: Write a Python function `def binary_search(sorted_lst: list[int], target: int) -> int` returning the index of target in a sorted ascending list, or -1 if absent. Reply with only one ```python code block.
items.tests:
assert binary_search([1,3,5,7,9], 7) == 3
assert binary_search([1,3,5,7,9], 2) == -1
assert binary_search([], 1) == -1

items.q: Write a Python function `def merge_intervals(intervals: list[list[int]]) -> list[list[int]]` that merges overlapping [start,end] intervals and returns them sorted by start. Reply with only one ```python code block.
items.tests:
assert merge_intervals([[1,3],[2,6],[8,10]]) == [[1,6],[8,10]]
assert merge_intervals([[1,4],[4,5]]) == [[1,5]]
assert merge_intervals([]) == []

items.q: Write a Python function `def caesar_cipher(s: str, shift: int) -> str` that shifts each lowercase a-z letter by `shift` positions (wrapping around), leaving non-lowercase characters unchanged. Reply with only one ```python code block.
items.tests:
assert caesar_cipher('abc', 1) == 'bcd'
assert caesar_cipher('xyz', 3) == 'abc'
assert caesar_cipher('a b!', 1) == 'b c!'

items.q: Write a Python function `def most_frequent(lst: list) -> object` returning the most frequent element; on ties return whichever tied element appears first in the list. Reply with only one ```python code block.
items.tests:
assert most_frequent([1,2,2,3]) == 2
assert most_frequent(['a','b','a','b']) == 'a'
assert most_frequent([5]) == 5

items.q: Write a Python function `def two_sum(nums: list[int], target: int) -> list[int]` returning a list of the two indices whose values sum to target (any valid pair), or an empty list if none exists. Reply with only one ```python code block.
items.tests:
r = two_sum([2,7,11,15], 9)
assert sorted(r) == [0,1]
assert two_sum([1,2,3], 100) == []

items.q: Write a Python function `def is_anagram(a: str, b: str) -> bool` that returns True if a and b are anagrams of each other (same character multiset, case-sensitive). Reply with only one ```python code block.
items.tests:
assert is_anagram('listen', 'silent') == True
assert is_anagram('abc', 'abd') == False
assert is_anagram('', '') == True

items.q: Write a Python function `def fib(n: int) -> int` returning the nth Fibonacci number using fib(0)=0, fib(1)=1, iteratively (no recursion required, but must be correct for n up to 30). Reply with only one ```python code block.
items.tests:
assert fib(0) == 0
assert fib(1) == 1
assert fib(10) == 55
assert fib(20) == 6765

items.q: Write a Python function `def matrix_transpose(m: list[list[int]]) -> list[list[int]]` returning the transpose of a rectangular matrix. Reply with only one ```python code block.
items.tests:
assert matrix_transpose([[1,2,3],[4,5,6]]) == [[1,4],[2,5],[3,6]]
assert matrix_transpose([[1]]) == [[1]]

items.q: Write a Python function `def run_length_encode(s: str) -> str` that run-length-encodes a string, e.g. 'aaabbc' -> 'a3b2c1' (each run always gets a count, even runs of length 1). Reply with only one ```python code block.
items.tests:
assert run_length_encode('aaabbc') == 'a3b2c1'
assert run_length_encode('') == ''
assert run_length_encode('abc') == 'a1b1c1'

items.q: Write a Python function `def valid_parentheses(s: str) -> bool` that returns True if the parentheses/brackets/braces in s are balanced and correctly nested ('()[]{}' pairs only; other characters are ignored). Reply with only one ```python code block.
items.tests:
assert valid_parentheses('([]{})') == True
assert valid_parentheses('([)]') == False
assert valid_parentheses('') == True
assert valid_parentheses('(a[b]{c})') == True

items.q: Write a Python function `def word_frequencies(text: str) -> dict` returning a dict mapping each lowercased whitespace-separated word to its occurrence count. Reply with only one ```python code block.
items.tests:
assert word_frequencies('The cat sat on the mat') == {'the':2,'cat':1,'sat':1,'on':1,'mat':1}
assert word_frequencies('') == {}
